items.title: 1. Long Vowels
items.description: Description: Long vowels. Prolong the vowel sound.

items.title: 2. Emphatic Consonants
items.description: Description: Emphatic (heavy) consonants. Deeper, guttural pronunciation, formed with the back of the tongue.

items.title: 3. Guttural H
items.description: Description: Guttural, rasping H (similar to the Spanish "Jota").
examples: Like the "j" in Spanish "José" or a strong, guttural breath.

items.title: 4. Guttural Sounds (Ayn/Hamza)
items.description: Description: Represents the Arabic letter 'Ayn (deep guttural sound) or Hamza (glottal stop).
examples: A slight contraction in the throat, like the sound between "uh-oh" in English.

items.title: 5. Guttural R
items.description: Description: The guttural R sound as in French.
examples: Like the "r" in French "Paris" or the Arabic "ġain" (ﻍ).

items.title: 6. Strong Guttural CH
items.description: Description: Strong guttural fricative (like the German "ch" in "Bach").
examples: Like the "ch" in German "Bach" or the Scottish "loch".

notes.title: Important Notes
items: Vowels "e" and "o": Rare in Classical Arabic. When you see them in transliterations, usually pronounce them as "i" and "u" respectively.
Auditory Learning: Perfect pronunciation requires listening to native Arabic speakers. This guide provides only approximations in English.
Consistency: The symbols are standardized to consistently represent the same Arabic sounds in all transliterations.
Unique Arabic Letters: Many Arabic sounds have no exact equivalents in English, hence the need for these special symbols.
Double Symbols: For some sounds, two different symbols may be used (e.g., ḫ and kh, š and sh), they represent the same sound.
